coredata.dc:title: Machine-learning analysis to predict the fluorescence quantum yield of carbon quantum dots in biochar
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Jiao
Zhang, Mengqian
Xu, Zijun
Ma, Ruoxin
Shi, Qingdong
coredata.dc:description: Biochar nanoparticles have recently attracted attention, owing to their environmental behavior and ecological effects. However, biochar has not been shown to contain carbon quantum dots (< 10 nm) with unique photovoltaic properties. Therefore, this study utilized several characterization techniques to demonstrate the generation of carbon quantum dots in biochar produced from 10 types of farm waste. The generated carbon quantum dots had a quasi-spherical morphology and high-resolution lattice stripes with lattice spacings of 0.20–0.23 nm. Moreover, they contained functional groups with good hydrophilic properties, such as amino and hydroxyl groups, and elemental O, C, and N on the surface. A crucial determinant of the photoluminescence properties of carbon quantum dots is their fluorescence quantum yield. Therefore, the relationship between the biochar preparation parameters and the fluorescence quantum yield was investigated using six machine learning analytical models based on 480 samples. Among the models, the gradient-boosting decision-tree regression model exhibited the best predictive performance (R2 > 0.9, RMSE <0.02, and MAPE <3), and was used for the analysis of feature importance; compared to the properties of the raw material, the production parameters had a greater effect on the fluorescence quantum yield. Additionally, four key features were identified: pyrolysis temperature, residence time, N content, and C/N ratio, which were independent of farm waste type. These features can be used to accurately predict the fluorescence quantum yield of carbon quantum dots in biochar. The relative error range between the predicted and the experimental value of fluorescence quantum yield is 0.00–4.60 %. Thus, the prediction model has the potential to predict the fluorescence quantum yield of carbon quantum dots in other types of farm waste biochar, and provides fundamental information for the study of biochar nanoparticles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biochar
Carbon quantum dots
Machine learning
Fluorescence quantum yield
Pyrolysis temperature
Farm waste

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Biochar production and carbon quantum dots extraction
Determination of hemicellulose, lignin, cellulose, and C/N ratio
Generation of carbon quantum dots in biochar assisted by machine learning
Generation of carbon quantum dots during biochar production
Construction of the machine learning model
Feature importance analysis and optimization of the machine learning model
Performance of machine learning models based on validation data

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Xingjiang Uighur Autonomous Region Talent Project

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the Tianchi Talent Program of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (No. 51052300544 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of machine learning-assisted CDs extraction in biochar. a) Extraction of CDs from biochar to establish a training dataset for machine learning. b) The QYs of CDs extracted from biochar were predicted with a machine learning model.

Characterization of CDs extracted from biochar from peanut hull (PH) and rice husk (RH). a) and b) TEM and HRTEM images. c) and d) FT-IR spectra. e) and f) XPS spectra. g) and h) Spectral characteristics of CDs (400 °C, 2 h).

Application of machine learning to predict the production of carbon quantum dots in biochar preparation. a) Feature importance retrieved from GBDT-R that learns from the full data set. b) Scatter plot of predicted and experimental QYs of 95 carbon quantum dots from GBDT-R.

Performance of the machine learning model on validation datasets. a) Feature importance extraction of pyrolysis temperature T, residence time t, N content, and C/N ratio based on the ranking importance algorithm. b) Scatter plot of predicted and experimental QYs of 95 CDs in GBDT-R based on the simplified features.
Overview of various machine learning-based studies in biomass pyrolysis.

row.entry: Multi-layer perceptron neural network, support vector machine, generalized additive model, and Gaussian process regression.
Feedstocks (C, H, N, S, and O content), volatile matter, fixed carbon, ash content, and operating parameters (biomass blending percentage, heating rate, reaction temperature, and reaction time).
Syngas yield, pyrolysis oil yield, and biochar yield.
Multilayer perceptron neural network, support vector regression, and random forest regression.
Sludge (C, H, N, S, and O content), proximate parameters (volatile matter, fixed carbon, and ash content), and pyrolysis reaction conditions (reaction temperature, heating rate, and reaction time).
Bio-oil yield, syngas yield, syngas composition, biochar yield, biochar characteristic.
Support vector regressor, random forest regressor, and gradient boost regressor.
Microwave pyrolysis operating conditions, biomass ultimate analysis, and biomass proximate analysis.
Bio-oil yield, syngas yield, syngas composition, biochar yield, and biochar characteristic.
Support vector machine and random forest.
C, H, O, N content, ash, hydrothermal reaction time, temperature, water content, pyrolysis temperature, and reaction time.
Comprising yield, energy recovery efficiency, and energy densification.
Temperature, reaction time, moisture content, heating rate, and sample mass.

head.ce:title: Machine-learning analysis to predict the fluorescence quantum yield of carbon quantum dots in biochar

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Ecology and Environment, Xin Jiang University, Urumqi 830046, PR China.
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Ecology and Environment, Xin Jiang University, Urumqi 830046, P.R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Energy Conservation and Environmental Protection Group, Beijing 100035, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: China Energy Conservation and Environmental Protection Group

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China Energy Conservation and Environmental Protection Group, Beijing 100035, P.R. China.

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Ecology and Environment, Xinjiang University, Shengli Road, Tianshan District, Urumqi 830046, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Jiao Chen and Mengqian Zhang contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biochar nanoparticles have recently attracted attention, owing to their environmental behavior and ecological effects. However, biochar has not been shown to contain carbon quantum dots (< 10 nm) with unique photovoltaic properties. Therefore, this study utilized several characterization techniques to demonstrate the generation of carbon quantum dots in biochar produced from 10 types of farm waste. The generated carbon quantum dots had a quasi-spherical morphology and high-resolution lattice stripes with lattice spacings of 0.20–0.23 nm. Moreover, they contained functional groups with good hydrophilic properties, such as amino and hydroxyl groups, and elemental O, C, and N on the surface. A crucial determinant of the photoluminescence properties of carbon quantum dots is their fluorescence quantum yield. Therefore, the relationship between the biochar preparation parameters and the fluorescence quantum yield was investigated using six machine learning analytical models based on 480 samples. Among the models, the gradient-boosting decision-tree regression model exhibited the best predictive performance (R2 > 0.9, RMSE <0.02, and MAPE <3), and was used for the analysis of feature importance; compared to the properties of the raw material, the production parameters had a greater effect on the fluorescence quantum yield. Additionally, four key features were identified: pyrolysis temperature, residence time, N content, and C/N ratio, which were independent of farm waste type. These features can be used to accurately predict the fluorescence quantum yield of carbon quantum dots in biochar. The relative error range between the predicted and the experimental value of fluorescence quantum yield is 0.00–4.60 %. Thus, the prediction model has the potential to predict the fluorescence quantum yield of carbon quantum dots in other types of farm waste biochar, and provides fundamental information for the study of biochar nanoparticles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The datasets generated during and/or analyzed during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biochar is a black powder rich in carbon and aromatic compounds with stable characteristics. It is produced through the pyrolysis of biomass (such as straw, forestry waste, animal manure, and agricultural residue) at low or high temperatures (<700 °C) and under oxygen-limited or anaerobic conditions (Kwapinski et al., 2010; Tripathi et al., 2016). Biochar production emits less CO2 than combustion and it reuses resources effectively (Woolf et al., 2010). Farm-waste biochar is widely applied to agricultural soils, and it can be used as a soil amendment to increase the efficiency of fertilizer and water use, promote plant growth, improve or prevent certain types of environmental pollution, and reduce greenhouse gas emissions (Atkinson et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2015; Woolf et al., 2010). Biochar has become the focus of considerable research interest, particularly as a means to achieve carbon neutrality in the agricultural, environmental, and energy domains. Recently, biochar production generated nanoparticles with high activity and mobility, and the environmental behavior, ecological function, and potential ecological risks of these nanoparticles have attracted research attention (Oleszczuk et al., 2016). However, it is unknown whether carbon quantum dots (CDs), which have unique photovoltaic properties, are generated during biochar production. Therefore, further investigation of biochar nanoparticles and the generation of CDs during biochar production is required.

CDs are novel zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, <10 nm in diameter, with fluorescent properties (Lim et al., 2015; Wang and Hu, 2014). They have excellent photoluminescence properties, good water solubility, and good biocompatibility and they are widely used in fluorescence detection (Xu et al., 2022b), photocatalysis (Wang et al., 2017), biological imaging (Hola et al., 2014), and plant nanofertilizers (Wang et al., 2019). It has been demonstrated that CDs can promote plant growth and improve resistance to salt stress. CDs synthesized by chemical synthesis can accelerate the photosynthetic electron transfer rate of rice leaves, promote plant photosynthesis, and ultimately increase the biomass of rice plants (Li et al., 2021). In addition, it has been shown that CDs synthesized from Salvia miltiorrhiza as biomass feedstock enhance crop adaptation to salt stress by enhancing Ca2+ signaling and Na+ homeostasis in the root system of Arabidopsis thaliana (Li et al., 2022a, 2022b). A crucial determinant of the photoluminescence properties of CDs is their fluorescence quantum yield (QY) (Chen et al., 2022). Therefore, it is necessary to investigate the characteristics and rules governing the QY of CDs in biochar prepared using different raw materials and process parameters.

With advancements in artificial intelligence, machine learning has been recognized as a powerful technique (Jordan and Mitchell, 2015). The potential of machine learning has been demonstrated in diverse fields such as chemical reaction monitoring and recognition (Bonk et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2022a), image and spectrum analysis (Enders et al., 2021), auxiliary illness detection (Zhu et al., 2021), and material performance improvements (Chen et al., 2022). Based on comprehensive data analysis, machine learning can provide effective learning models to extract hidden information from enormous volumes of data from experiments and computational simulations (Jordan and Mitchell, 2015). In recent years, the application of machine learning to biomass thermal conversion processes has attracted great interest (Table 1 ). The most commonly used machine learning methods for biomass thermal conversion processes are neural networks, random forest regression, and support vector machine regression. Most studies use biomass properties and pyrolysis conditions (temperature, heating rate, and residence time) as inputs to the machine learning model, and the yield and composition of the thermal conversion products (bio-oil, biochar, and syngas) as the target variables of the study (Shahbeik et al., 2023, Shafizadeh, 2022; Yang et al., 2022). The assistance of machine learning techniques enables process optimization, yield prediction, and real-time monitoring in the thermal conversion of biomass to bioenergy (Cao et al., 2016; Li et al., 2020; Shafizadeh, 2022). However, the application of machine learning to predict the QY of CDs generated during the production of biochar from biomass has not yet been investigated.

Therefore, inspired by the considerable potential of machine learning in biomass thermal conversion processes research, this study used machine learning to investigate the CDs production characteristics and rules during farm-waste biochar production. This study had four main objectives: to establish whether CDs were generated during biochar production; to explore the laws and mechanisms governing the QYs of CDs generated using various materials and conditions; and to determine whether machine learning could be used to predict the CDs generated in biochar. Furthermore, a database was established to record the QYs of the CDs, providing a basis for the application and promotion of biochar in the future. To the best of the authors' knowledge, this is the first attempt to use machine learning techniques to predict the QY of CDs generated during the production of biochar from biomass. Moreover, owing to the widespread use of farm waste, a considerable shift in research interest regarding potential applications of biochar in agricultural production is expected.

10 types of farmland waste were purchased from Qingdao Lianfeng Agricultural Products Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, China): wheat straw (WS), corn straw (CS), bamboo stalk (BS), rice straw (RS), rice husk (RH), peanut hull (PH), peanut straw (PS), green bamboo stalk (GBS), corn cob (CC), and rape stalk (RST). Sulfuric acid was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and quinine sulfate was purchased from Macklin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade, purchased from commercial suppliers, and used as received without further purification. Deionized water was supplied by China Agricultural University (Beijing, China). Dialysis bags (MWCO 2000 Da) were purchased from BioTopped Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biochar production and carbon quantum dots extraction
ce:section.ce:para: The biochar production and CDs extraction processes are illustrated in Fig. 1a. Biochar was produced using each type of farm waste, which were all rich in lignocellulose, easy to access, and commonly disposed as biomass. The wastes were collected, rinsed, dried, and ground into fine powders with particles <0.2 mm in diameter. These raw materials were then heated at a rate of 10 °C·min−1 and pyrolyzed at 300, 400, or 500 °C for 2, 4, 6, or 8 h under limited‑oxygen conditions. Each group had 4 replicates, affording a total of 480 samples (Table S1).

The samples were allowed to cool naturally to the ambient temperature, followed by sonication with deionized water (1:50 w/w) at 40 kHz for 120 min and constant stirring for 24 h. Next, the solution was extracted and centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min. The suspension was filtered through a polyethersulfone membrane filter (diameter: 0.22 mm) and the filtrate was collected and then dialyzed for 2 h using a dialysis bag (MW:2000 Da). Finally, the dialyzed solution was subjected to vacuum drying and a CDs powder was obtained.
The synthetic procedure used in this study was straightforward and adhered to the principles of green chemistry without using any inorganic solvents or generating any toxic byproducts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of hemicellulose, lignin, cellulose, and C/N ratio
ce:section.ce:para: A modified version of the method reported by Qu et al. (2011) was used to extract the cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin from the farm wastes. Details of the extraction process are provided in the Supplementary Information (SI). A CN elemental analyzer (Leco, USA) was used to determine the total nitrogen (TN), total carbon (TC), and C/N ratios.

A fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) was used to obtain the fluorescence spectra and a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Tu-1810, General Analysis, China) was used to obtain the UV spectra. The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using an FT-IR spectrometer (iS10, Nicolet, USA) and the KBr pellet method of sample preparation. An advanced data acquisition and processing system was used to analyze the X-ray data (Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher, USA) to maximize the amount of extracted information. The morphology and microstructure of the CDs were measured using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR- TEM; FEI Talos F200S, 200 kV, Thermo Fisher, USA).

An aqueous solution of quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 with a QY of approximately 54 % was used as a standard reference solution. The corresponding absorbance values A of solutions with different concentrations were determined using a 350-nm UV light; A should be between 0.01 and 0.05. By scanning the fluorescence spectra of the reference and sample solutions, the fluorometer software could determine the integrated fluorescence intensity (i.e., the area of the fluorescence spectrum). To calculate the QYs of the CDs Φ
X , the refractive indices of deionized water and 0.1 mol·L−1 H2SO4 were measured and substituted into Eq. (1).
(1)
Φ X
=
Φ ST
k X
/
k ST
η 2
X
/
η 2
ST
where Φ
ST denotes the QY of the reference material; k
X and k
ST denote the slopes of the linear fitting lines of the sample and reference materials, respectively; and η
X and η
ST denote the refractive indices of the sample and reference materials, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of carbon quantum dots in biochar assisted by machine learning
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1b, machine learning was used in the generation and extraction of CDs from biochar and this process can be divided into two steps: First, 11 important parameters were identified from the biochar-production experiment, including the type of farm waste (class); cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin, ash, moisture, N, C, and C/N contents of the samples; pyrolysis temperature T (300, 400, and 500 °C); and residence time t (2, 4, 6, and 8 h). 10 types of farm waste commonly used to produce biochar were selected as raw materials. All the raw materials contained cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin, which are rich carbon sources; hence, the N, C, and C/N contents of the raw materials were key features that affected the QY of the CDs. Thus, 480 CDs samples were extracted from the experiments with different combinations of preparation parameters.

The QY of each CDs sample was measured and the dataset of the CDs samples was randomly divided into training and validation datasets with a ratio of 8:2. The model was trained using the training dataset and a preliminary evaluation of its effectiveness was conducted using the validation dataset (Tang et al., 2020). The optimized model accurately predicted the QY of the CDs. A database containing the QYs, raw-material properties, and biochar-production parameters of all the CDs samples and machine-learning predictions was compiled for use in future studies on the generation of CDs during farm-waste biochar production.

The data were analyzed and processed using Pearson's correlation analysis and machine-learning regression. Pearson's correlation coefficient describes the linear correlation between two variables. The regression models included decision-tree regression (DT-R), random-forest regression (RF-R), gradient-boosting decision-tree regression (GBDT-R), extra-trees regression (ET-R), K-nearest-neighbor regression (KNN-R), and XGBoost regression (XGBoost-R). The GBDT-R model is an additive model that trains a set of classification and regression trees in series and then sums the predictions of all the regression trees to obtain a strong learner. Each new tree fits the negative gradient direction of the current-loss function. Finally, the sum of the regression trees is used as the output regression result. The GBDT-R model was established using the training dataset. Then, the feature importance was calculated using the established GBDT-R. Finally, the established GBDT-R model was applied to the training and testing datasets to evaluate the model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of carbon quantum dots during biochar production
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies of the CDs obtained from each type of farm-waste biochar were characterized using TEM, as shown in Figs. 2a,b and S1. All the samples exhibited quasi-spherical morphology with a uniform particle size distribution and an average diameter of 1.69–2.85 nm (Table S2). Moreover, the HR-TEM images showed that CDs had high-resolution lattice stripes with lattice spacings of 0.20–0.23 nm.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to confirm that various functional groups were present on the CDs. As shown in Figs. 2c,d and S2, peaks corresponding to NH or OH vibrational stretching were observed at approximately 3400 cm−1, indicating numerous amino and hydroxyl groups on the surfaces of the CDs and therefore good hydrophilicity (Atchudan et al., 2018). Peaks indicating the presence of CH and CC groups were observed at approximately 2950 and 1400 cm−1, respectively (Ma et al., 2017; Mu et al., 2019; Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015), and peaks corresponding to the CO groups were observed at approximately 1630 cm−1 (Kurniawan and Chiang, 2020; Sachdev and Gopinath, 2015).

As shown in Figs. 2e,f and S3, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the elements present on the surfaces of CDs. The XPS full-scan spectra showed peaks corresponding to O 1s, C 1s, and N 1s at approximately 532, 286, and 400 eV, respectively. This suggests that the CDs were mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen (Shangguan et al., 2017; Yan et al., 2016).

The optical properties of CDs were also investigated. The excitation and emission spectra were determined using fluorescence spectroscopy and fluorescence images were obtained using a UV lamp. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at different excitation wavelengths and the maximum excitation wavelength was determined. The maximum excitation and emission peaks of CDs occurred at approximately 300 and 400 nm, respectively, as shown in Figs. 2g,h and S4. The CDs solutions were colorless under natural light and emitted intense blue fluorescence under UV light. Based on the morphological and structural analyses, it was revealed that farm-waste biochar contained CDs.

The UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were used to determine the QY of CDs generated at different pyrolysis temperatures (300, 400, and 500 °C) and different residence times (2, 4, 6, and 8 h). The pyrolysis temperature had a significant effect on the QY of the CDs, which decreased as the pyrolysis temperature increased (Fig. S5). This contrasts with the results reported by Zhao et al. (2020) who artificially synthesized green fluorescent CDs with high QY using biomass raw materials, where the QY of the CDs increased as the reaction temperature increased. This result occurred as the unitary emission center increased owing to the combination of oxygen-containing groups (C=O) and conjugated carbon atoms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the machine learning model
ce:section.ce:para:
The Pearson correlation coefficient is used in this study to determine the correlation between each feature and the target variable for feature selection. It determines the correlation between various features to reveal the relative importance of each feature to the target variable (Li et al., 2022a, 2022b). As shown in Fig. 3a, the correlations between pairs of features were calculated, with low linear correlations confirming the validity of the feature selection. Furthermore, the study evaluated six regression models (DT-R, RF-R, GBDT-R, ET-R, KNN-R, and XGBoost-R) using the nested cross-validation method to determine the optimum QY based on the model's predictions. The models were evaluated using three widely recognized performance metrics: the coefficient of determination (R 2), root mean square error (RMSE), and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE). The closer the R
2 is to 1, the higher the accuracy of the model. RMSE is the square root of the mean squared error, which reflects the difference between the predicted and actual values; the smaller the value, the higher the accuracy of the model. MAPE is the percentage value of the mean absolute error, which reflects the actual situation of the prediction value error. The smaller the MAPE value, the higher the accuracy of the model (Han et al., 2020). The results were summarized and compared using box–line plots, as shown in Fig. 3b. The GBDT-R model (R
2 > 0.9, RMSE <0.02, and MAPE <3) outperformed the other models. This finding is attributed to the high prediction accuracy of the GBDT-R model compared to other models. The GBDT-R model is a potent prediction model created by merging various decision tree models; it is resilient to missing values and outliers, is not readily influenced by extreme values, and can handle high-dimensional sparse data (Yang et al., 2022). Therefore, the GBDT-R model was selected as the best model for this study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feature importance analysis and optimization of the machine learning model
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the black-box nature of machine learning models, feature importance analysis is necessary for machine learning modeling (Shahbeik et al., 2022). Therefore, the GBDT-R model was used for feature importance assessment to select the features that affect the prediction results (Fig. 4a). Features of greater importance had a stronger effect on the experimental results. This analysis indicated that the pyrolysis temperature had the strongest effect on the QY of the CDs, followed by the residence time, N content, and C/N ratio. It has been reported that the relative contribution of pyrolysis conditions to biochar yield is higher than that of biomass properties, and the pyrolysis temperature has the greatest influence on biochar yield (Table S3) (Shahbeik et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2019). In addition, previous studies have shown that the QYs of CDs prepared via chemical synthesis are affected by parameters such as the reaction temperature, precursor mass, ramp rate, and reaction time (Han et al., 2020). Therefore, it is reasonable to assume that the process parameters in this study, pyrolysis temperature and residence time, would significantly affect the QY of the CDs in biochar. Moreover, the N content and C/N ratio are basic properties of farmland waste, which can significantly affect the QY of CDs. For example, Hu et al. (2020) artificially synthesized nitrate-doped CDs from green tea residue using high-temperature pyrolysis. The prepared CDs had a QY of 14.8 %, which was more than three times that of the undoped CDs. This occurred because nitrogen doping affected the energy-state density of the CDs, which enhanced their fluorescence-emission intensity. Finally, the study revealed that the type of farmland waste had minimal effect on the QY of the CDs, which indicates that the GBDT-R model for predicting CDs in biochar can be generalized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of machine learning models based on validation data
ce:section.ce:para: The results showed that the GBDT-R model was best for predicting the QYs of CDs in biochar. The GBDT-R model predicted 96 more validation sets, as shown in Fig. 4b. The predicted (y) and experimental (x) values of QY were uniformly distributed on the function image with y = x. Additionally, the relative error range between the predicted and the experimental QY values was 0.00–9.75 %. To provide predictions for future studies, the feature parameters were simplified and four feature values were selected (pyrolysis temperature T, residence time t, N content, and C/N ratio) that had a feature importance >10 % for further validation. The importance of the four feature values was extracted from the trained GBDT-R model (Fig. 5a). The relative error range between the predicted and the experimental QY values was 0.00–4.60 % when the GBDT-R model employed these four eigenvalues to predict 96 validation datasets, as shown in Fig. 5b. This indicates that the QY of CDs generated during farm-waste biochar production can be predicted using these four simple eigenvalues.

In this study, it was demonstrated that CDs were generated when 10 different types of farm waste were used to produce biochar. Furthermore, a machine-learning strategy was developed that successfully predicted the QY of CDs generated during biochar production. From this, a prediction model and database for the QY of CDs in farm-waste biochar was established. Initially, 11 features from the raw-material characteristics and process parameters were selected as input features for the machine learning model and 480 CDs samples were extracted from biochar to produce the QY dataset. Then, six machine-learning models (DT-R, RF-R, GBDT-R, ET-R, KNN-R, and XGBoost-R) were tested. The GBDT-R model showed remarkable prediction accuracy with an R
2 higher than 0.9, and the statistical errors (MAEP and RMSE) were remarkably low in predicting the QY of CDs in biochar. Next, the feature importance was analyzed using the GBDT-R model, and it was determined that the biochar-production process parameters had the greatest effect on the QY, exceeding the effects of the raw-material properties. Moreover, the type of farm waste had no significant effect on the QY. Thus, the model was simplified and four features (pyrolysis temperature, residence time, N content, and C/N ratio) were determined to have the greatest effect on the accuracy of the QY prediction. Due to the comprehensive utilization of agricultural biomass waste, the finding that CDs are generated during biochar preparation will lead to a significant shift in research interest for various potential applications of biochar in agricultural production. Furthermore, the method proposed in this study can be used as a basis for the development of new techniques using artificial intelligence to analyze and predict CDs generated during biochar production. However, the eco-environmental effects of CDs in biochar remain to be investigated, especially in agricultural soils.
This work was financially supported by the Tianchi Talent Program of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (No. 51052300544).

Jiao Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Mengqian Zhang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Zijun Xu: Investigation, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Ruoxin Ma: Investigation, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Qingdong Shi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support provided by the Tianchi Talent Program of Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region (No. 51052300544).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2023.165136.
